identity.title: Research and Development in the Physical, Engineering, and Life Sciences (except Nanotechnology and Biotechnology)

lens.included_activities: Independent physical, engineering, and non-biotechnology life-science R&D firms that sell recurring or repeat research, development, testing, engineering, or laboratory programs to external customers and can fall in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive corporate, university, government, and nonprofit research units; biotechnology and nanotechnology R&D; prototype manufacturing; one-off firms without repeat outsourced customer work; and financing shells.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External commercial or government customers buy project, task-order, retainer, or repeat-program research and development services; work is commonly specialist labor- and facility-intensive.

a.bridge: S2 measures the 2023 occupation and wage mix for NAICS 541700, which includes 541715 plus biotechnology and social-science R&D. I apply that mix to 541715 and count documentation, coding, analysis, literature synthesis, scheduling, proposal, and administrative work as AI-exposed while excluding much experimental design, physical experimentation, safety, and accountable technical judgment. The broad source likely overstates exposure for the target because its biotechnology and computer-intensive components may have more digital task content than the excluded target activities.
a.rationale: The proxy industry has 922280 workers; life, physical, and social science occupations are 23.16 percent, computer and mathematical 14.29 percent, management 20.28 percent, and business and financial operations 12.14 percent. High-wage scientific and engineering work has material AI assistance potential, but the core experimental and accountable portions are not substitution-ready.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 541715 payroll-by-SOC extract from Lightcast or BLS microdata, paired with task interviews across engineering laboratories, contract research firms, and government R&D contractors, would permit a wage-weighted task decomposition.
caveats: No published six-digit occupational mix was located.
Exposure is not realized labor removal and excludes work already automated.
The source includes biotechnology and social-science R&D outside the target.

rho.rationale: A five-year rollout can implement AI in software, documentation, search, data handling, and proposal workflows, but validation, IP and export-control restrictions, regulated quality systems, experimental reproducibility, and customer acceptance constrain deployment. Observed Claude use is still substantially augmentative, supporting a below-majority implementation estimate.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of 541715 firms measuring production deployments, FTE avoided, validation requirements, and restricted-data usage, plus vendor implementation case studies, would materially change this range.
caveats: This is a five-year judgment, not a measured adoption rate.
Adoption differs sharply between computational engineering and wet-lab or secure government programs.

e.rationale: The code covers many external R&D laboratories and services, but it also contains captive-like and project-specific activity that does not meet the repeat-outsourced-service lens. The Census definition spans aerospace, agriculture, electronics, engineering, environmental, medical, and other laboratories, so no single observed eligibility share is defensible.
e.change_evidence: Dun & Bradstreet or PitchBook firm-level NAICS 541715 records with customer concentration, recurring-revenue, employee, ownership, and EBITDA fields would allow the lens filters to be counted directly.
caveats: No public dataset classifies 541715 firms by repeat outsourced service and normalized EBITDA.
NAICS establishment classification does not identify captive status or customer concentration.

s5.bridge: S5 measures US small-business owners' stated plans in the next five years, not completed qualifying control transfers and not 541715 firms. I convert the broad intent signal into a lower completed-transfer probability for eligible lower-middle-market R&D firms; stated plans likely overstate completed transferable sales because technical founder dependence, security clearances, customer consents, and shutdowns prevent some exits.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that owners aged 55 or older were more likely to plan sale or transfer in five years than younger owners, 17 percent versus 10 percent. A qualifying external control transfer should be less frequent than that planning rate, yielding an 8 percent central estimate.
s5.change_evidence: GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, and business-broker closing records filtered to 541715 and the specified EBITDA band, together with Census owner-age microdata, would directly measure this probability.
caveats: Owner intent is not a transaction close rate.
Public transaction marketplaces underrepresent clearance-dependent and highly technical R&D firms.

q.rationale: Custom research can retain part of a workflow productivity gain under fixed-price, milestone, or capacity-constrained engagements, but R&D's labor-heavy, custom nature limits durable capture. Federal guidance says cost-reimbursement is usually appropriate when R&D specifications and cost estimation are uncertain, which passes labor savings through to a customer on that material channel.
q.change_evidence: A stratified review of 541715 commercial MSAs and federal contract records, coding fixed-price, cost-reimbursement, time-and-materials, renewal, and gain-sharing terms, would measure retention directly.
caveats: The FAR governs federal procurement, not the whole industry.
No public revenue-weighted contract-type mix exists for the frozen lens.

d5.bridge: S8 projects employment in the broader Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services sector to rise 7.5 percent from 2024 to 2034. I use approximately half of that decade trend as a five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand proxy for the current 541715 service basket; it could understate R&D demand if research budgets outgrow the sector or overstate it if R&D is displaced by internal teams or software.
d5.rationale: The central case is modest real demand growth rather than a step change. Business R&D remains large and 5417 has a 21.5 percent R&D-to-sales ratio among R&D-performing companies, but that evidence does not isolate the target code or outsourced demand.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-dollar revenue and contract-award series for 541715 from the Census Annual Services Survey, USAspending, and commercial customer procurement panels would replace the sector proxy.
caveats: Employment growth is not a direct constant-price demand measure.
Business R&D figures include internal performers and companies outside the target code.

o.rationale: Most year-five demand should still require a responsible technical operator to formulate questions, run or oversee experiments, validate findings, manage facilities, and bear contractual or regulatory accountability. AI can remove some self-service analysis and lower-complexity research support, so the estimate is below one.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and win-loss data separating purchases of operator-led R&D from software-only simulation, analysis, and self-service tools would test the operator-required share.
caveats: No source measures replacement of 541715 services by software-only offerings.
The estimate varies by discipline, with computational work more substitutable than laboratory and field work.

narrative.executive_view: This is a broad, talent-intensive R&D service industry whose recurring outsourced operators can benefit from AI in information and workflow work, but the physical, judgment-heavy research core limits direct labor substitution. Public evidence is stronger for the broad 5417 group than for the target six-digit code.
narrative.ai_impact: The broad 541700 occupation mix is concentrated in science, engineering, computing, management, and business operations. AI can accelerate coding, documentation, literature synthesis, data handling, project administration, and proposal work, while experiments, quality control, safety, and technical sign-off remain human-accountable.
narrative.value_capture: Value retention is constrained by bespoke customer work and a federal R&D channel where cost-reimbursement is usually appropriate. Fixed-price and milestone projects can retain more productivity, but repeat customers can seek lower prices or greater scope at renewal.
narrative.firm_availability: Census classifies a wide array of laboratories and services in 541715, yet public data do not isolate lower-middle-market firms with recurring external revenue. Broad small-business evidence signals some five-year owner-transition intent, but technical founder dependence makes completed qualifying transfers less certain.
narrative.demand_durability: Demand should remain moderately durable because customers still need accountable research and development, and the broader professional-scientific sector is projected to grow. The projection is only a sector proxy, while internalization and software-enabled self-service can reduce outsourced volumes in some niches.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main uncertainty is code heterogeneity and sparse six-digit operational data. Results may differ markedly among defense engineering, environmental research, medical research, and computational R&D; restricted data, IP, validation, and clearance requirements can both protect demand and slow implementation.
narrative.principal_driver: High-skill knowledge workflows create a meaningful but bounded AI implementation opportunity alongside continuing need for accountable technical delivery.
narrative.principal_weakness: Six-digit evidence on outsourced recurring revenue, LMM transfer closings, contract terms, and task mix is unavailable, leaving key values dependent on broad-industry proxies and estimates.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS 541715 classification details
sources.supports: Defines the target code and enumerates included R&D laboratory and service activities.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Scientific Research and Development Services
sources.supports: Reports a 922280-worker NAICS 541700 occupation and wage distribution, including 23.16 percent life, physical, and social science, 14.29 percent computer and mathematical, 20.28 percent management, and 12.14 percent business and financial occupations.

sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index: New building blocks for understanding AI use
sources.supports: Reports observed Claude task speedups by task complexity and a 52 percent augmentation versus 45 percent automation split in its November 2025 Claude.ai sample.

sources.title: Business R&D Performance in the United States Increases to $722 Billion in 2023
sources.supports: Reports 2023 business R&D totals, 5417 external funding figures, and a 21.5 percent R&D-to-sales ratio for scientific R&D services among R&D-performing companies.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports fall-2024 US small-business owner plans over the next five years, including sale or transfer intent of 17 percent for owners age 55 or older and 10 percent for younger owners.

sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report
sources.supports: Describes a national small-business sale and listing dataset covering about 50000 businesses and reports market transaction activity.

sources.title: FAR 35.006 Contracting methods and contract type
sources.supports: States that cost-reimbursement contracts are usually appropriate for R&D because uncertain specifications and costs normally preclude fixed-price contracting.

sources.title: BLS Employment Projections 2024-2034
sources.supports: Reports 7.5 percent projected employment growth for Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services from 2024 to 2034.

sources.title: Introducing LifeSciBench
sources.supports: Describes real life-science research as interpretation of incomplete evidence, experimental design and troubleshooting, translational-risk evaluation, and deciding next steps.
